The latter could be a passive to amadal above (``pushed away, rejected''?), but winaḍal seems to be a different word. For the meaning I propose tentatively: ``to bring forth, utter'' (conn. with wĕḍar, q.v.?).

citations.text: 10.12: gĕtih iṅ rwan iṅ waduri;

127.32: dukutnya kakajar mwaṅ pakis, waduri kaywanya (in a cemetery). Acc. to KBW, bal. mandori and manduri (van Eck mĕndure) is ``a certain plant with broad leaves, Calotropis gigantea; the gum is supposed to remove the remains of a thorn in the flesh ... Manduri reges, an item of clothing of a special pattern, worn in the gambuh. In Ad it renders Skt arkapattra, ``the leaf of the Arka plant'', the arka being in fact ``the plant Calotropis gigantea (the larger leaves are used for sacrificial ceremonies)''. The last comment is relevant to L 37.3. From PYñ and KY we might conclude that it is a creeper. The waduri is found in the afterworld and hence also in cemeteries (ST, CA, and there looks more like a tree). KBW s.v.) waduri also gives: oya maṅĕb riṅ waduri maron tuṅgal, oya maṅĕb iṅ tahĕn widara kapanasan (Atmaprasaṅsa.). ``This plant in hell owes its name to the double meaning of tapana in ancient India: hell, and Calotropis gigantea''. In MW, however, we find tapana: sun (Mhbh, RY etc), heat (lex), name of a hell (Mn. Buddh.), also name of various plants, i.a. = tapanacchada (the sunflower, lex). Calotropis gigantea is not mentioned. Has there been influence from, or confusion with, wadara, widara, bidara, q.v.?

definition: 1. ritual requisites (offerings etc) for ceremonial occasions (wedding, installation, etc); also: the whole ceremony, feast. It is in all probability Skt bali (see bali II), but also very similar to pali-pali. See also raja-, sarwa-. 2. cloth, dress, drapery, prob. for ceremonial use or reserved for certain persons. See also sarwawali. 3. also of persons: he who is in charge of the wali? (cf walyan); or: has a ceremonial function?

definition: (Skt) ``act or object of choosing'', choice, wish, boon, gift, favour; ``select'', valuable, precious, best, most excellent among 1. gift, favour, boon. Also wara sih, wara karuṇa; and see dewatā-, kṛta-, labdha-, parama-, wacana-. 2. select, eminent, precious etc.; in compounds before and after the qualified word. Before an adjective wara is used with the meaning ati-, mahā-. See also dwija-, giri-, hrū-, kawi-, kuṭa-, maharṣi-, muni-, prawara, priya-, ṛṣi-, śara-, sura-, taru-, yati-. 3. It is frequently used before personal names of young men and women (cf dyah, ratna)

definition: (Skt any real (existing) substance, thing, object; the real; the right thing, worthy object; goods, wealth, property; subject-matter, theme). See also alpa-, bhuwana-, nir-, rāja-, sarwa-, awastuka. 1. thing, object, matter; something, anything, everything 2. reality, the really existing things 3. really, in reality, undoubtedly, surely, indeed, in fact, actually (often = prasiddha, q.v.; cf also paramārtha)

There is prob. a mutual influence; cf ModJ estu. A substantival use, followed by -niṅ, is very common, meaning ``the real content of, the actual nature of, the true meaning of'':

7.164: baṅ-baṅ wetan puṅgawa adan aseba, ramya lumintaṅ margi lĕpas iṅ wataṅan amĕpĕki paṅastryan. From the preceding passages it appears that wataṅan is the place where the king gives audience. Emerging from the pura (jro, kaḍatwan) he takes his place there. From AgP it seems that it is a building of wood. But the courtyard is certainly also meant; it has sand, gates; a wrestling-match is held there; in the centre is a maṇḍapa. It is possible that sthāna wataṅan (Udy;  BY;  AbhW) and bwat wataṅan (KY) indicate the building in the middle (cf AWj 3.3: bwat mantĕn ... muṅgw i tĕṅah iṅ wataṅan), which has given its name to the whole area. The bal. com. regard it as the equivalent of jaba tĕṅah and wijil piṇḍo.

KBW s.v.) pamĕṅkaṅ: bal.com.'s rendering of wijil pisan. GR: name of two gates, to the east and the west of the pagĕlaran. Pig. JNHW: 1. dial. obs. interspace; 2. dial. planted courtyard. v. 131.1: dwijāṅśa madulur-dulur maṅabhiwāda para wiku marĕk maṅañjali; the following passages from Sum give an image of the wiku in court society: 28.11: wiku haji jĕnĕk aṅher iṅ śūnyâlawas amarat (?) / wruh aṅaji sira santoṣâṅdoh-dohi narapati / nĕhĕr amarahi sampun wṛddhâṅgöṅ ulah alaṅö / atuha-tuha kadi brĕm santĕn kāku manis ira. (12) Sira ta marĕk aluṅguh milw iṅ dāyaka humatur / kinasĕgĕhan apan wruh ṅūnîṅ rājya sira kabeh / apituwi paḍa rowaṅ-rowaṅ niṅ maṅikĕt-ikĕt / mukha niṅ aṅucap aśrī de niṅ mojar akakawin: (there follow 14 stanzas with a description of the patapan in the different seasons); (27) nahan ucapan ira mpuṅkw aṅde harṣa ri saṅ Aja / wĕṅi mara sakamantyan prāptâneka taḍah ira / dwijawara sira mālih muṅgw iṅ bwat alu tĕpi lor / para wiku juga kāry āgoṣṭhi mwaṅ nṛpatanaya; (28 and 29 music and dancing);

42.15: yan kāṅĕn sukha niṅ licin dumadak ahyun adaluwaṅa harṣa riṅ jamaṅ / denya n kilya rarā makāśraya nareśwara kumĕmita kāla niṅ rare / yan wṛddhâjara dharma riṅ giri#wanâmagĕhakĕna ri dharma saṅ wiku / ramya ṅgwan guruloka sarwa tĕka yan lumaku-laku rinampak iṅ kili;

48.1: ya ta tĕmahana tiṅlur denya n śaiwa sira siwin / ikaṅ apalara n anwam wrūheṅ śāstra wiku haji / lilu tumakula lampūn dan yan paṇḍita nagara / atuha-tuha bagañjiṅ byaktâṅhöbi yan inusir; (2) kunaṅ amuhara kiṅkiṅ yan bot swan sipi mararas / wiku mirah aguṇâśrī maṅgĕh wikwa narapati / sira ta siwin ... ;

49.5: lawan-lawana ni ṅhulun wiku mirah lituhayu pinakesi niṅ kuṭi / manisnya kadi poh apādapa tiki n mamaragi gula gaṇṭi dug siṅī / liriṅnya pinarantyan i ṅhulun iṅ ambyan iṅ umah i sĕḍĕṅ nikânasi / dudū swara nike karinya karĕṅö panamaya nika yan pare ṅhulun;

159.4: saṅ hulu kĕmbaṅ anak wĕsi haji sira milw anadin wiṣaya / tan tuna riṅ paribhāṣa sira dahat alĕp nira yan paṅucap ... ; (5) kweh nira saṅ bhawika prakaśita milu wikw anusup riṅ alas / bhakti tuwi n katĕkan tuha masa niṅ awalkala nora hiner / gīta lawan kakawin binisa pinakacaṇḍi <ni>reṅ nagara / maywan akīrti palambaṅ amanis alaṅö hibĕk iṅ patani; (6) wuṅkuk aṅambĕkakĕn kawi milu kawilaṅ kawi wikw alawas / sabhya jitākṣara hasyakaraṇa pinakādi ni kawruh ikā / lumbu walūh ajujöṅ rasa ni manis i lambaṅ ikā rinasan / ṅhiṅ rwa tiwas dumadak lagi winaca wawâṅrahatîṅ amaca;

This could be Indonesian Skt for ``enforcement of the ritual prescriptions'', but it is also possible that wipati means ``to drink'' (from Skt pibati?) in accordance with later bal. usage (see KBW s.v.), so that it would mean ``the rules for drinking''.

107: āpan kweh wiṣamanya kewala tikaṅ gaty atry (gatya tri?) ajar ni ṅhulun (see atri, under tri I). The three wiṣama metres are treated in Wṛt 101-103; then in 104: ri tĕkanta riṅ kahanan i ṅwaṅ inutus ira saṅ karîṅ purī / moga kadi tinitah iṅ madana / wiṣama tri tan sipi kaharṣa niṅ hiḍĕp. Wiṣama tri is an allusion to the three metres. But what does it mean in the sentence? Wiṣamâtri, ``thrice difficult or dangerous'' (cf biṣama), referring to tĕkanta? Or ``causing inconvenience and shouting (see tri II) to be satisfied'', referring to kaharṣa, the love of the poet? 2. hard to traverse, difficult, dangerous. See biṣama and ati-.

definition: 1. (Skt) sphere (of influence or activity), dominion, kingdom, territory, domain 2. (Skt) scope, range, reach, field of action, object, any object of affection (concern, attention), esp. the objects (field) of the five senses 3. (Skt) everything perceptible by the senses, what holds the senses, the visible world; what arouses sensual desire, sensual enjoyments. See also (for 1.2.3.): biṣa-, bhoga-, brahma-, daṇḍa-, kāma-, māṅsa-, netra-, nir-, pañca-, rāga-, sparśa-, strī-, swa-, thāni-. 4. affection, passion 5. having a passion for, keen on 6. skilled (in). Perhaps influence of bisa ya, see bisa.

definition: (Skt distinction; peculiar mark, speciality; kind, species; peculiar merit, excellence, superiority) distinguished from others, with its special qualities; eminent, excellent, outstanding, best, highest, superior, paramount, supreme, ruling supreme. See also a-, eka-, mahā-, parama-, sarwa-.

sub_entries.definition: (grammatically the most satisfactory meaning seems to be: for whom one ``amulaṅ'', and hence perhaps: (the women) for whom one has girded oneself up for war (i.e. has donned spurs like a fighting-cock).

citations.text: 25.15: muwah wwaya sirâhalĕp rara sujanma pinawulaṅakĕn hane paṅuṅaṅan.

bal. bulaṅ is an item of women's ceremonial attire, a strip of cloth about fifteen feet long bound around the torso. Cf also ModJ bolaṅ, and Malay bulaṅ (Wilk.): `` ... its commonest use is to define the process of binding the artificial spur (taji) to a fighting-cock's foot, this being done by means of a thread about 6 feet long''. There appears to be an association with sampir (Sum 59.6; SD 4.7), another piece of clothing.

definition: The basic meaning of wyat seems to be: a lasting impression or effect or interest. It seems to be close to rahat, q.v., which is often used in bal.com. as a rendering, and connected with ModJ awet, ``lasting, durable''. See also wiyat.

sub_entries.definition: Nearly always (the only exception Nag 53.1) with negation: tan pawyat, tan hanâwyat: ``without suffering a lasting impression'', untroubled, unconcerned, undismayed, undaunted; (also:) without anyone's being concerned, without ado, just so
